segments.text:
Requirements for strand elongation
1)  A free 3’-OH at the end of the strand to be extended
2)  A hydrogen-bonded (base-paired) nucleotide at the end of
the strand to be elongated
3)  An unpaired base on the adjacent nucleotide in the template
strand
So how does it ever get started?
DNA polymerases can do strand elongation, but not strand initiation

RNA polymerases can do strand initiation
in prokaryotes, origin binding proteins recruit RNA Polymerase
makes first primer
all other primers = primase, recruited by helicase

first primer
made by RNA pol
this is in prokaryotes – one circular chromosome
one origin of replication
elongation by
DNA pol
helicase recruited
to expose more
template

each helicase travels with
a primase
2nd helicase and primase
start second replication fork
ssb = single-strand binding protein

DNA pol III stops at 5’ end of
previous Okazaki fragment – no template
RNA in DNA
nick in backbone

DNA polymerase I can remove and replace primers
“nick translation”
5’  3’ exonuclease activity
removes a nucleotide from the 5’ end of a strand
nucleotide to be removed must have a free 5’ phosphate
nucleotide to be removed must be base-paired
nucleotide being removed can be a ribonucleotide or a deoxyribonucleotide
process only removes one nucleotide at a time

DNA polymerase enzymes
DNA Pol I and DNA Pol III used in replication
BOTH polymerases have:						ONLY Pol I has:
strand elongation – 2-step reaction				5’  3’ exonuclease activity
cleave phosphate bond in dNTP				“nick translation”
catalyze new bond formation
ATP hydrolysis for translocation
3’  5’ exonuclease activity
“proofreading”
alternates 5’3’ exonuclease activity and polymerase activity

initial primer at origin
made by RNA pol
origin binding
proteins
gyrase
(downstream)

DNA polymerase enzymes
DNA Pol I and DNA Pol III used in replication
BOTH polymerases have:						ONLY Pol I has:
strand elongation – 2-step reaction				5’  3’ exonuclease activity
cleave phosphate bond in dNTP				“nick translation”
catalyze new bond formation
ATP hydrolysis for translocation
3’  5’ exonuclease activity
“proofreading”

Klenow enzyme (Klenow fragment) =
DNA pol I without 5’  3’ exonuclease activity subunit
Klenow
DNA pol I holoenzyme

Klenow enzyme
DNA pol I holoenzyme

replication of SMALL circular DNA
phage genomes
VERY fast
doesn’t fit any model (conservative,
semi-conservative, dispersive)
Rolling circle replication
concatemer
lagging strand synthesis

Klenow keeps extending 3’ end
endonuclease
cleaves concatemer
into 1-copy units

Additional complications for eukaryotes
Prokaryotes – small genomes  (106 – 107 bp)
one chromosome/cell
circular DNA
Eukaryotes – genomes are much larger  (109 – 1010 bp)
multiple chromosomes/cell
DNA is in nucleosomes
chromosomes are linear
prokaryotes – one origin of replication
Eukaryotic chromosomes – multiple origins of replication

DNA polymerases are slow (500-5000 bp/min)
DNA must be unwound from histones before
helicase can separate strands
Nucleosomes form as DNA is replicated

Eukaryotic chromosomes are linear
DNA exonuclease removes unpaired DNA
(NOT an exonuclease activity of DNA pol)
primer at 5’ end
removed by RNase H

chromosomes get shorter
every round of replication
sets limit on number of times
a cell can divide

Telomeres
structural DNA at ends of chromosomes
short, highly repetitive sequence

Telomerase = ribonuclear protein complex
DNA polymerase activity
uses internal RNA as template
telomerase extends unreplicated DNA

newly made DNA is template for primase
lagging strand synthesis
RNase H removes primer
DNA 3’  5’ exonuclease removes
unpaired DNA
no loss of genetic information
